title: Hand-fitted marbled endpapers
subtitle: Measuring, trimming, and pasting marbled sheets into a finished binding
excerpt: Marbled endpapers are the most visible decorative element in a bound book, wrapping from the inside board onto the first and last leaves of the text block. Getting them square, trimmed to the right size, and pasted without bubbles or cockling is a separate skill from making the marbled sheet in the first place.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on paste-down endpapers and decorative papers. Internet Archive scans available. Specific measurements and technique details are consistent with Cockerell's descriptions of fitting paste-downs and compensating for paper pull.

glossaryTerms.definition: The half of an endpaper glued to the inside face of the cover board, hiding the raw board edges and the turn-ins of the covering material.
The free half of an endpaper, which faces the first or last page of the text block and is not glued to the board.
The covering material (cloth or paper) folded over the edge of a board and glued to its inside face.
Waviness or buckling in paper caused by uneven moisture uptake when adhesive is applied to one side only.

content.text: Fitting marbled endpapers into a casebound book is the last step before the book goes under the press to dry. It looks straightforward but the paper is already mordanted and marbled, which means it responds differently to water-based adhesive than plain cartridge does. Get it wrong and the
paste-down
cocks up at the corners or the
fly-leaf
waves when the book is opened. This technique walks through measuring, cutting, and pasting the marbled sheet to prevent both.
Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Keep the non-cutting hand behind the blade's path when trimming.
What you need

Marbled sheet: an alum-mordanted sheet with a flat-dried finish, at least 10 mm larger on all four sides than the opened book board. Marbled sheets that were dried face-down on a flat surface and not curl-affected work best.
Adhesive: acid-free PVA thinned to a slightly runny consistency. Methyl cellulose mixed into the PVA at a ratio of 1:4 extends the open time, which is useful when fitting large sheets.
Boards: the casebound book must be fully constructed with the text block sewn and the cover cloth applied, but not yet pressed with endpapers in.
Measuring and cutting
Open the book and place the marbled sheet face-down on the open front board. Mark the board dimensions on the sheet in pencil with a 2 mm margin beyond the board edge on the three sides that show the
turn-in
of the book cloth.
Cut the marbled sheet to the marked dimensions on the cutting mat. Hold the ruler firmly and cut in a single firm pass.
Check the cut sheet fits inside the open board with the 2 mm margin hidden under the turn-in on all three sides. Adjust if needed before pasting.
Pasting and positioning

Place the marbled sheet face-down on clean waste paper. Apply PVA from the centre outward to the edges in a thin, even coat. The sheet will begin to curl slightly toward the pasted side. Work quickly.
Pick up the sheet and place it face-up on the open board, tucking the 2 mm margins under the turn-ins. Smooth from the spine edge outward, pushing any air pocket toward the fore-edge.
Lay a clean piece of wastepaper over the pasted side and rub firmly with the bone folder from centre to corners. This presses the adhesive into contact and removes any remaining air.
Immediately close the book with a clean sheet of wastepaper between the paste-down and the first text-block leaf. Place in the book press, clamping evenly across the full board.
Repeat for the back endpaper.
Leave under press for a minimum of 2 hours, ideally overnight.
Cockling
during the drying phase is almost entirely prevented by the press.
Troubleshooting

attrs.symptom: Paste-down lifts at the corners after drying
attrs.cause: Insufficient adhesive at the corners, or corners not pressed firmly enough during fitting.

content.text: Apply a small amount of fresh PVA under the lifted corner with a fine brush, press firmly, and replace under a weight overnight.

attrs.symptom: Board warps inward after endpaper is pasted
attrs.cause: Adhesive caused the board face to shrink; the back of the board was not balanced with a compensating paste-down.

content.text: Paste the back endpaper immediately after the front and clamp both boards simultaneously in the press. The tension from both sides cancels out.

attrs.symptom: Marbled pattern shows brush marks through the paste-down
attrs.cause: PVA applied too heavily; the moisture broke down the alum bond in the marbling.

content.text: Use a thinner, faster-drying mix of PVA, and apply it to the board rather than to the marbled sheet to reduce the moisture the sheet absorbs.
Variations

The same technique applies to paste-paper endpapers (cooked-paste sheets with combed or sponged patterns). Paste paper is more absorbent than marbled paper and needs slightly less diluted PVA. For a book with exposed boards, consider tipping a strip of marbled paper onto just the first text-block leaf rather than pasting down a full endpaper.
